utterances: (SOUNDBITE OF RALLY)
(Foreign language spoken).
And Remy Stern, I wonder, have you ever fallen for the wiles of the pitchman?
I have.
I have.
I purchased a Popeil product myself back in the day, the Ronco Pasta Maker, which was a great infomercial.
And Ron makes chocolate pasta at the very end.
That's sort of the big moment there, which, you know, wowed me.
And I had never even thought of chocolate pasta.
And of course, you know, I had to run to the phone to buy it.
And I bought a few other products over the years and found myself sort of strangely amused and entranced by these weird shows that would come on late at night with these weird, funny characters.
And it, you know, turns out to be a booming business as well.
We're talking with Remy Stern, editor and publisher of the Web site CityLife.com, and author of the new book ""But, Wait…There's More!"" 800-989-8255, email: talk@npr.org.
What have you bought from a pitchman?
And let's go to Madeleine.
Madeleine with us from Brockton, Massachusetts.
Hi.
Thanks for taking my call.
Go ahead, please.
I am laughing so hard at this because I almost bought - I actually cancelled it after I ordered it because I came to my senses.
But I almost bought - it was a brush attached to something that went round and round and round really fast and made your hair magically, beautifully silky and shiny and straight.
(Soundbite of laughter)
And this attracted you?
Oh, my goodness, they had - these women on there that had the most wildest, most out-of-control hair that I've ever seen.
And after five or 10 minutes with this brush, they were transformed into, you know, Demi Moore, like (unintelligible) with this beautiful long, straight hair.
And…
And I realized I was probably going to be committed to buying gallons and gallons of whatever shampoo you needed to make your hair this way with this brush.
And Remy, that's part of what's going on here.
There's a little bit more going on.
They continue to try to sell you additional stuff once they get you on the phone.
Yeah.
That's sort of the gold mine for them is they either sell you the same product again and again, so they sign you up for sort of the monthly payment plan.
So you get the Proactiv acne solution or the DVD Time Life DVD series, for example, and a new one comes to you every month.
That's sort of how they make a lot of money.
Or they sell you a product and, you know, you have to buy replacement materials for it constantly.
The, you know, hair product market is a funny one.
I think my favorite is the Flowbie, which was an attachment to your vacuum cleaner that you could actually use to cut your hair.
That was a funny one from about 20 years ago.
The same kind of category is kind of - remain popular…
Recycled…
…year after year.
Yeah.
Many of them - many of the products aren't actually new.
The biggest product of the year is actually the Snuggie, which is based on a product called the Slanket, which came along a few years ago which no one's ever heard of, of course.
But then the Snuggie came along and for some reason was a huge sensation.
Madeleine, is your hair okay now?
It's perfectly fine.
(Soundbite of laughter)
Okay.
With the Clairol product or whatever it is you use.
That's right.
That's right.
It works just fine.
All right, Madeleine.
Congratulations, and happy brushing.
Thanks a lot.
Bye.
Here's an email from Robin in Elkhart, Indiana.
Do I have the kitchen gadgets and the other stuff?
I crumble in the wee hours of the morning when I can't sleep.

Along the way, I've collected the Jack LaLanne juicer - never used; Swivel Sweeper, which I love; X Cooker, which I wore out; Mighty Putty, which is still in the box; closet organizer hangers, which I found very useful; core dance exercise videos that are still in their shrink wrap; any number of other oddments rarely used and of which most of which is going into my garage sale next week.
These are garage sale staples, aren't they?
They are.

And then he actually makes an interesting point, which is that, you know, late at night we're very susceptible to these pitches and it's something that the infomercial producers realized at the very beginning, which is that our defenses come down in the wee hours of the morning and we're a little sleepy and we're a little bit more prone to the power of suggestion.
And that's of course when they - these commercials, these shows come on.
And then we suddenly find ourselves at 3:00 in the morning ordering a beef jerky machine for some strange reason.
And then we wake up the next day and we wonder why we just did that.
What will I do with all that beef jerky?
Yes.
Yeah.
Or the caller before, I guess, had the sense to cancel the order.
Cancel, yeah.
Let's see if we can go next to Jonathan.
Jonathan calling us from Tulsa.
Yeah.
Hi.
I am.
I actually bought a Ron Popeil food dryer.
I remember you talking about the beef jerky just now.
And that thing actually works really great.
It's got removable sections and everything.
And I've had it for years now and it's wonderful.
But I think, you know, like you were saying, at 3:00 in the morning, something about Ron Popeil just - you know?
He's figured out the brain patterns, I think, of the insomniacs.
I guess.
I guess.
Jonathan, you still use the food dryer though?
Absolutely.
It comes in handy during the summers, actually, when I started drying stuff from my garden, so…
Hmm.
All right, well, continued - happy food drying.
Thanks a lot.
Elaine in Lancaster, New York writes: We purchased the Shark vacuum carpet sweeper.
Of course everything works on TV.
And this little item looked too good to be true.
We have two bull terriers and the hairiest cat in the world, which is why we wanted the sweeper.
And it works great.
The charge lasts up to 45 minutes.
The little hopper that holds the dust/dirt is easily emptied.
It was worth the $60 we paid.
And I guess we do have to remember that, Remy - sometimes these things actually do work fine.
Oh, there's some great products that are available on television.
It's very much sort of hit or miss.
There are some great things.
There are some not-so-great things.
There are some outright frauds.
It's really buyer beware.
You know, you're well advised to do a little research beforehand, before you buy.
But…
At 3:00 in the morning?
How are you going to do that?
Anyway, there are two kinds of pitchmen too.
There are the shouters, like the Billy Mays, and the more suave approach.
There are different approaches.
And they've experimented with different types of pitchmen over the years.
One popular pitchman that you see on the air is the English pitchman, which is because they figured out that English accents sound more authoritative to American audiences.
And they're actually very successful at selling us things, so they brought over all of these English pitchmen.
And because we can't tell the difference between an English or Australian accent, or most people can't, they've been - brought over Australian pitchmen as well.
So you'll see, for example, the Magic Bullet has an Australian pitchman, or Anthony Sullivan, who is Billy Mays's partner in crime there, is from England.
And so that's been another approach.

There's a lot of psychology that goes on behind the scenes of infomercials that we're not even aware of, which is what I found so fascinating, is that they've really figured out these formulas to get us to spend as much money as we do.
And it's not as haphazard or as cheesy or as silly as we probably think just watching these shows.
Remy Stern is the author of the book ""But, Wait…There's More!"" We're talking about pitchmen, following the unfortunate death earlier this week of Billy Mays.
